network.short_description: Interactive graph showing the four-layer supply chain for a selected technology: Technology → Component → Material → Country.
network.long_description:
The dependency network renders the full Shallow Technology Dependency Network (STDN) for a single technology as a concentric-ring graph. Each node belongs to one of four layers:

• Technology (center): the end product being analyzed
• Component (inner ring): primary manufacturing subassemblies (e.g., Display Module, Battery)
• Material (middle ring): raw materials or processed inputs required by each component
• Country (outer ring): nations that produce each material

Edge types:
• HAS_COMPONENT (solid, Technology → Component): the technology contains this component
• USES_MATERIAL (solid, Component → Material): constituent material physically incorporated into the component
• CONSUMES_PROCESS_MATERIAL (dashed purple, Technology → Material or Component → Material): process consumable used during manufacturing but not in the final product
• PRODUCED_IN (solid, Material → Country): the material is produced in this country, with percentage share on the edge

Node styling:
• Amber nodes = constituent materials
• Purple nodes = process consumable materials
• Node size reflects confidence score

Selecting a node highlights its connected edges and neighbors, dimming unrelated nodes. The detail panel shows confidence scores, production shares, HS codes, and dependency type.
network.provenance: The STDN is generated by a multi-agent AI pipeline (dpi_stdn_agentic). Stage 1 decomposes technologies into components via multi-agent debate (3 agents, 3 rounds). Stage 2 identifies constituent materials via multi-agent debate with ontology matching against HS codes. Stage 2b extracts process consumables via single-agent extraction with judge review. Stage 3 maps materials to producing countries using USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries (2022–2025) where available, falling back to multi-agent LLM consensus where authoritative data is unavailable. Component and material names are normalized to canonical vocabularies for cross-technology consistency. Each edge carries a confidence score (0–1) reflecting the extraction agent's certainty, and a provenance tag (USGS or LLM) indicating the data source for country production shares.

material_network.short_description: Interactive graph showing which technologies depend on a selected material, organized hierarchically: Material → Domain → Subdomain → Technology.
material_network.long_description:
The material dependency network inverts the perspective of the technology network: instead of asking 'what does this technology need?', it asks 'who needs this material?'. The graph renders as a four-layer concentric ring with the selected material at the center.

Layers (center outward):
• Material (center): the raw material or process consumable being analyzed
• Domain (inner ring): the three technology domains — Microelectronics, Biotechnology, Pharmaceuticals
• Subdomain (middle ring): 12 subdomains per domain (e.g., Consumer Electronics, Clinical Diagnostics, Oral Solid Dosage), clustered around their parent domain
• Technology (outer ring): individual technologies that use the material, clustered around their subdomain

Interaction:
• Click a domain node to focus on that domain — other domains disappear and the selected domain's subdomains spread across the full circle
• Click a subdomain to drill further — the material, domain, and subdomain stack vertically and the subdomain's technologies (up to 5) fan out at larger size
• Click the material node or the background to step back one level
• Double-click a technology node to navigate to the Technology Network tab for that technology

Technology nodes carry a rolled-up component count showing how many components within that technology use the selected material. This is visible in the detail panel on click.
material_network.provenance: The material network is constructed from the same STDN dataset as the technology network, queried across all three domains simultaneously. Each technology's appearance is determined by whether any row in the dataset links that technology to the selected material (through any component or as an assembly-level process consumable). Component counts are computed by counting distinct non-empty component names per technology for the selected material. The domain and subdomain hierarchy is derived from the original technology classification used during dataset construction (12 subdomains × 5 technologies per domain).

concentration.long_name: Material Market Concentration by Country: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) Score
concentration.short_description: Measures how concentrated a material's production is among countries, using the sum of squared market shares (HHI). Higher scores mean fewer countries control supply.
concentration.long_description:
The Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) quantifies market concentration for a material's producing countries.

Formula:
  HHI = Σ(sᵢ²) for i = 1 to N
where sᵢ is the production share (as a percentage, 0–100) of country i, and N is the number of producing countries.

Interpretation:
• HHI = 10,000: single-country monopoly (one country produces 100%)
• HHI = 5,000: two countries each produce ~70/30 split
• HHI = 2,000: five countries with roughly equal shares
• HHI ≈ 0: production is highly fragmented across many countries

HHI is computed per technology-material pair. For each pair, the system takes the maximum production share per country (across components) to avoid double-counting when a material is used by multiple components in the same technology.

Classification thresholds:
• Extreme (≥ 5,000): near-monopoly, critical supply risk
• High (≥ 2,500): highly concentrated, significant risk
• Medium (≥ 1,500): moderately concentrated
• Low (< 1,500): diversified supply base

The Extreme tier (HHI ≥ 5,000) is added beyond the standard DOJ/FTC thresholds to flag near-monopoly conditions common in critical mineral markets (e.g., rare earths, cobalt).
concentration.provenance: Country production shares are sourced from the USGS Minerals Yearbook (2022–2025) where available, with LLM-inferred multi-agent consensus estimates used as fallback for materials not covered by USGS. HHI threshold classifications for Extreme, High, and Medium follow the U.S. Department of Justice and Federal Trade Commission Horizontal Merger Guidelines (2010, §5.3), adapted for supply chain analysis with the addition of an Extreme tier.

sources.name: U.S. Department of Justice & Federal Trade Commission

dominance.short_description: Ranks countries by the number of materials for which they are the world's top producer, indicating potential single-point-of-failure leverage over supply chains.
dominance.long_description:
Dominance analysis identifies countries that act as systemic chokepoints in global supply chains.

Key definitions:

• Top producer: For a given material, the country with the highest production share across all technologies in the dataset. The 'Other Countries' aggregate is excluded.

• Dominated material: A material for which a specific country is the top producer. For example, if China produces 60% of Gallium and no other country exceeds this share, then Gallium is a 'dominated material' for China.

• Dominance count: The number of distinct materials a country dominates (i.e., is the top producer for).

Additional metrics per country:
• Technologies affected: number of distinct technologies that depend on materials produced by this country
• Materials produced: total number of distinct materials this country supplies (not just dominates)
• Average share: mean production share across all material-technology pairs involving this country
• Max share: highest single production share held by this country for any material

Dominance classification thresholds:
• Critical (≥ 10 materials dominated): extreme systemic leverage
• High (≥ 5): significant chokepoint
• Moderate (≥ 2): notable presence
• Low (< 2): limited dominance
dominance.provenance: For each material in the dataset, the country with the maximum production share across all technology supply chain entries is identified as the top producer. The 'Other Countries' aggregate is excluded from dominance calculations. Production shares are sourced from the USGS Minerals Yearbook (2022–2025) where available, with LLM-inferred multi-agent consensus estimates as fallback. Dominance threshold levels are defined for this tool to distinguish countries posing systemic supply chain risk from those with limited leverage. The dominated materials list includes a dependency type indicator (constituent vs. process consumable) to distinguish physical incorporation from manufacturing consumption.

overlap.long_name: Material Overlap Across Technologies: Systemic Risk
overlap.short_description: Identifies materials and countries shared across multiple technologies, revealing systemic dependencies where a single supply disruption can propagate across the technology portfolio.
overlap.long_description:
Overlap analysis quantifies systemic risk by identifying shared dependencies across the technology portfolio.

Key definitions:

• Material overlap: A material that appears in the supply chains of 2 or more technologies. The overlap count is the number of distinct technologies that depend on the material.

• Country overlap: A country that supplies materials to 2 or more technologies. The overlap count is the number of distinct technologies the country's production supports.

• Systemic risk multiplier: A material or country with high overlap count is a risk multiplier — disruption to a single supply source cascades across many technologies simultaneously.

Metrics per shared material:
• Technologies: number and list of technologies sharing this dependency
• HHI: concentration score for this material (same formula as Concentration tab)
• Top producers: countries with the largest production shares
• Dependency type: constituent or process consumable

Metrics per shared country:
• Technologies: number and list of technologies this country supplies
• Materials: number and list of materials this country produces
• Average share: mean production share across all supply relationships

Overlap risk classification:
• High (≥ 6 technologies): critical systemic dependency
• Moderate (≥ 4): significant cross-technology exposure
• Low (≥ 3): notable shared dependency
• Minimal (2): limited overlap
overlap.provenance: Overlap counts are computed from the full STDN dataset by grouping supply chain edges by material (or country) and counting distinct technologies. Only materials or countries appearing in 2+ technologies are included. HHI scores for shared materials use the same methodology as the Concentration tab (sum of squared country shares per material). The 'Other Countries' aggregate is excluded from country overlap analysis. Production shares are sourced from USGS Minerals Yearbook (2022–2025) where available, with LLM-inferred estimates as fallback.

trade_disruption.short_description: Disruption analysis based on actual UN Comtrade trade flows (US imports by value). The disruption heatmap shows which countries, if removed from trade, cause the greatest loss of import value for each material bucket.
trade_disruption.long_description:
DISRUPTION HEATMAP: For each material and year, the dashboard finds the set of k countries whose removal would cause the maximum loss of US import value. The per-year disruption score is the fraction of total import value lost (e.g., if the US imports $100M of a material and $40M comes from one country, removing that country scores 0.40). Each heatmap cell shows a composite percentage that combines disruption intensity with consistency: composite = (average disruption score across years the country appeared in the top-k set) x (fraction of years the country appeared). A high percentage means the country is both a major import source and a persistent one. Countries are sorted by total composite risk across all materials.

SUBSTITUTABILITY: For each material and k value, the substitutability view counts how many distinct countries have appeared in the top-k supplier set across all years. The number shown is the count of distinct countries. The bar width and color both represent the ratio of distinct countries to the maximum possible (k x number of years). Red bars (ratio 0.30 or below) indicate high lock-in, where the same few countries dominate year after year. Amber bars (0.30 to 0.60) indicate moderate rotation. Green bars (above 0.60) indicate frequent supplier turnover and high substitutability.
trade_disruption.provenance: Trade flow data is sourced from the UN Comtrade database via the Comtrade API, filtered to US imports by value. Material-to-HS-code mappings are derived from the STDN dataset. Disruption sets are computed by exhaustive enumeration of all k-country subsets and selecting the subset whose removal maximizes lost import value. Based on Vullikanti et al. (2026).

disruption.short_description: Simulates the impact of removing a country from the global supply base, showing which technologies, components, and materials are affected and how severely.
disruption.long_description:
The disruption simulator models a hypothetical scenario in which a selected country's entire production capacity becomes unavailable.

For each technology, the simulator computes:

• Materials affected: all materials where the disrupted country is a producer
• Components affected: all components that depend on affected materials
• Max share lost: the highest production share (%) that the disrupted country holds for any single material in this technology's supply chain
• Top producer count: number of materials for which the disrupted country is the #1 global producer

Severity classification per technology:
• Critical: max share lost ≥ 50% OR top producer for ≥ 3 materials. The technology would face severe manufacturing disruption with no readily available alternative supply.
• High: max share lost ≥ 30% OR top producer for ≥ 1 material. Significant impact requiring supply chain adjustments.
• Moderate: max share lost ≥ 10%. Noticeable but manageable impact with existing alternative suppliers.
• Low: max share lost < 10%. Minimal direct impact.

The simulator provides a three-level drill-down:
1. Technology level: severity, total materials/components affected, max share lost
2. Component level: which components use affected materials, per-component share loss
3. Material level: specific material, share percentage, whether disrupted country is top producer

Both constituent materials and process consumables are included in severity calculations — a manufacturing line can be shut down equally by loss of a constituent material or a critical process gas.
disruption.provenance: Disruption impacts are computed from the full STDN dataset by filtering all supply chain edges involving the selected country. Top-producer status is determined by comparing the country's production share against all other producers for each material (excluding the 'Other Countries' aggregate). Production shares are sourced from USGS Minerals Yearbook (2022–2025) where available, with LLM-inferred multi-agent consensus estimates as fallback. Severity thresholds are defined for this tool to distinguish critical single-source dependencies from lower-impact exposures.

analyst.short_description: Template-driven structured reports and AI-assisted chat for interpreting supply chain risks across the STDN dataset.
analyst.long_description:
The Policy Analyst provides two modes of analysis:

1. Structured report templates (6 available):
• Supply chain risks for [technology]: HHI concentration analysis, critical materials, top producing countries
• Disruption impact of [country]: affected technologies and materials by severity tier
• Highest concentration risks: cross-technology HHI analysis identifying systemic chokepoints
• Country dominance of [country]: dominated materials, technologies affected, market share analysis
• Cross-technology shared materials: overlap analysis with systemic risk assessment
• Disruption impact of [material]: technologies dependent on the material, producing countries, concentration risk, and mitigation recommendations

Each structured report generates sections with risk-level indicators (Critical/High/Moderate/Low/Info), quantitative metrics, bulleted findings, and actionable recommendations. Reports are generated deterministically from the STDN dataset — no LLM is used at runtime.

2. AI chat (Ask Gemini):
Freeform questions answered by Gemini 2.5 Flash using the full STDN knowledge graph as context, including node metrics (PageRank, in-degree), edge attributes, and cross-technology relationships.
analyst.provenance: Structured reports are generated deterministically from the same STDN dataset and API endpoints used by the other tabs. All metrics (HHI, dominance counts, overlap counts, disruption severity) use the same formulas described in their respective tab descriptions. AI chat responses are produced by Google Gemini 2.5 Flash with the STDN knowledge graph injected as context; these responses are LLM-generated interpretations and should be cross-referenced against the structured analysis tabs for verification.
